CURRENT AFFAIRS,

The Belfast Northern Wiig, of Thursday last, devoted
its leading article to a bigot yell. Some people have
been treading on the intolerant toes of one of the most
exclusive corporations in Ireland—the Great Northern
Railway.  When base “Idolators” dare to protest
against tyranny, injustice and intolerance, the Saved”’
bigots jump up with fire in their eyes, and denounce
the “Idolators’’ for—what do you think—for persecu-
tion! The Whig has managed to crowd an uncommon
lot of misstatements into its screech,

Tet us take the following :— Only a few weeks ago,
when a private Bill was promoted in Parliament for
improving the drainage of Rathmines, the Nationalist
members destroyed it, and for no other reason than that
the bulk of the ratepayers in Rathmines are Protestants,
who are not prepared to obey the marching orders of
(Cardinal Logue or Archbishop Walsh.” As a matter
of fact the Bill was one for, amongst other things, the
annexation of Terenure, a proposal that the Dublin
County Council strenuously opposed. ‘We cannot say

otly how the ratepayers are divided, but so far as

opulation goes, the “ Idolators,” we believe, are in the
jori The Rathmines Council, though it has

jority of Catholics, is a notorious hot-bed of bigots, and
could almost beat the Great Northern Railway itself
for its exclusive dealing in the matter of official posi-
tions carrying salaries.

The Northern Whig grows bolder in its misstatements
when it comes to the Great Northern Railway. Evi-
dently the toes of that Railway’s friends are smarting
very much from the heels of some of the Irish members.
Referring to the Irish railways, the Whig says : —“Their
sin is that they will not appoint Roman Catholics to
positions irrespective of other considerations.””  Our
readers will not be surprised at this statement in view
of the source from which it emanates. Of course the
demand that is made in connection with the Great
Northern Railway is simply that the clerkships be
thrown open to competitive examination. The manage-
ment of the Great Northern Railway are afraid of com-
petitive examination for their clerkships, and refuse to
establish them; and by that very refusal they practi-
cally admit, what is evident on the face of the revela-
tions of fact which we published, and which are con-
tained in “Three Railways and a Bank,”’” that the
whole system is a nest of bigotry and exclusive dealing
in paid appointments.

With a wild yell that shows how badly Ascendancy’s
toe is hurt by the action of Mr. MacVeagh and others
in the British Parliament, the Northern Whig screeches :
—“They will not rest satisfied unless every official from
the manager to the railway porter has the approval of
Mother Church.”

The Northern Whig remarks that “in face of abuse
and threats, the directors of the priucipal railways have
refused to be coerced.”” Of course the public facts are
that as a result of a strong and well ordered campaign
for some modicum of Justice, Tolerance, and Fair Play,
both the Midland Railway and the Great S. and
‘W. Railway have thrown open—with some reservation
—their clerkships to competitive examination. Tt is
now sought, and very justly, to induce the Great
Northern Railway to do likewise, and until they do it
they will get trouble in Parliament every time they
want to pass a Bill.

“ We have no desire,”’ says the pious Northern Whig,
“to see religious differences accentuated in Ireland.” Of
course it hasn’t, if only the poor “ Idolators’’ would
remain content to keep their necks humbly under the
heels of Freemasons, Orangemen, and other types of
bigots! If the “Idolators’’ would stand and deliver
up everything—even Belfast Corporation scavengerships
—then papers like the Belfast Northern Whig will do
nothing to accentuate religious differences in Ireland!
“But,”” our mild contemporary goes on, “ But we must
warn those who are instigating and carrying on this
Sectarian propaganda, that if they persist in their
foolish course, there will be reprisals.”’  We spy enter-
tainment ; we see Wade Thompson, the pervert Priests’
Protector, with a sword in one hand, a torch in the
other, his face blue-black with holy indignation, as he
shouts to, say, the persecuted staff of the Royal Dublin
Society, and tells them of the foul attempt to “ex-
terminate ’ the Protesbants by establishing competitive
oxamination for clerkships in the Great Northern Rail-
way! We can see the look of awe on the faces of the
agsombled staff of the Royal Dublin Society as they are
asked the dread question—HHow many of you would be
here enjoying fat jobs if you had to stand up in open
competition to: the treason-breathing “ Idolators?” %Ve
fear the screeches of the Northern Whig will not succeed
in keeping back the tide of progress. Catholics want
no fayours, they do not wish to tread on the rights of

THE LEADER.

any other people. But they want justice and fair play
for themselves. They want open competition for
olerkships in such public corporations as the Great
Northern Railway; and the impious and despairing
howl that the Northern Whig has sent up is a pretty
fair indication of what goes on in the absence of such
open safeguards.

‘We have a strong faith in dynamic force as well as in
ethics. It is all very well to show up tho bigoted
sereeches of the Northern Whig for what they are worth.
But the bigots can laugh at our rights so long as they
practically monopolise the power. The best reply to
such intolerant screeches from Orange Ulster is for the
persecuted majority to rally to the side of Tolerance,
Justice and Fair Play, by joining the Catholic Defence
Society. The subscription to the Central branch is only
10s. yearly, and Catholic fighters for Tolerance, Justice,
and Fair Play in any part of the country are free to
join the Central branch. The offices are at No. 11
Lower Dominick Street, Dublin. The need of this
Society is eloquently demonstrated by the eruption of
higotry, misrepresentation, and threats which the effort
to establish competitive examination, as the means of
obtaining clerkships in the Great Northern Railway,
has occasioned.

We have before us a printed advertisement circular
from an English firm that deal in Sanctuary oil.
Nearly half the circular is taken up with “ unsolicited
testimonials.””  Seven of these ‘ unsolicited testi-
monials ’’ are from clergymen in Ireland, but a reader
of ours, who brought the matter under our attention,
probably did mot notice that every oune of these ““un-
solicited testimonials ” from Irish Clergymen, is dated
1899, the year before the starting of the Lzaper. Bub
we can scarcely say much to clergymen or others who
gave ““unsolicited testimonials’’ to a British oil firm
previous to the appearance of this paper.

Replying to Mr. Hazleton, Mr. Winston Churchill
stated that the Canadian Government paid £1 bonus to
booking agents for each Irish person of certain classes
emigrating to the country of snow. He declined, evi-
dently, to act on Mr. Hazleton’s suggestion that repre-
sentations should be made to the Canadian Government
to discontinue these bonuses. There you are. Unfor-
tunate Ireland has to pay for the Imperial Army and
Navy; it is scandalously over-taxed, and emigration
would naturally be an outcome of such over-taxation.
The snow country, Canada, pays nothing for the Im-
perial Army and Navy, and out of the spare cash she
offers £1 a head for certain classes of emigrants from
TIreland, a country from whence many people are pre-
disposed to emigrate owing to the enormous and unjust
burden of taxation. Ireland rears a human being up to,
say, 20 years of age, at a cost, to make a moderate esti-
mate, of £200; she does that in face of enormous over-
taxation and political disabilities.  Canada, of the
snows, has to pay nothing for the Imperial Army and
Navy, so she sends over £I for a human being that
has ‘cost over-taxed Ireland £200 to raise. It is a
prefty arrangement!

Mr. Swift MacNeill has been prying into Sir Horace
Plunkett’s directorships. Sir Horace, by way of ex-
planation, has stated that tho companies of which he is
director “wero purely private companies in which his
interest was small,,and in which his position was mainly
fiduciary.”” ~ We have looked up the matter.  Sir
Horace is Clhairman of the Pelton Steamship Co., Ltd.,
the registered offices of which are at Milburn House,
Newcastle-on-Tyne. This “ private company,”” of which
* Sir Horace is Chairman—a rather “small interest "’ by
* the way—has an authorised capital of £1560,000 in

shares of £10, and £115,000 has been subscribed and

paid up. Thero is also 5 per cent, Debenture Stock to

tho tune of £25,000. That makes an issued capital of

£140,000 for this “private company’’ in which Sir

~ Horace as Chairman has a “small ”’ interest. Perhaps
‘one of our Newcastle readers would call at the regis-

ciple! The other “private’’ company, in which ir
Horaco has a small interest, is John Bower and Part-
ners, Ltd., registered office also at Milburn House, New-
castle-on-Tyno.  This “ priva_(',c ’f company hash :ix;)
authorised capital of a little triflo in the region of half-
a-million—to be precise, £554’780‘. We' do not know
the amount of “small*” interest which Sir Horace may
have in this British enterprise. Is it not a wonder that
such an Irish agricultural and m;lustrlal enthusiast
would not put his money in industrial ventures in TIre-
land, and become Chairman of some Irish company, even
though it were a “ private’’ company?  Might he not
sell out of the “ Pelton Steamship Co., Ltd.,”” and 'put
the proceeds into the Irish boot factory on American
lines, which Mr. Hayden is busy in promoting.

Whatever the Temperance Society that benefited by
the All Treland Bazaar may be doing, or not doing, and
whatever thoy may be doing with the funds—by the
way, we seo that the Secretary, Mr. Moriarty, is a
bankrupt—certainly the cause of temperance is gaining
ground steadily. The conference betwoen representa-
tives of various trades and labour bodies and other
societies that met last week in the City Hall, under the
presidency of Father Aloysius, was a gathering that
really meant something, and Father Aloysius in no way
overstated the matter when he said that the Temperauce
Movement, had come to stay, and would brook opposi-
tion from no quarter. Mr. J. Farrell, Chairman of the
Trades’ Council, spoke bed-rock truth when he said
that the cause of temperance was the workingman’s
cause.

The Deputation to the Lord Lieutenant was also
significant if the result was not very satisfactory.
His Grace the Archbishop of Dublin, in the letter which
he wrote to Sir Andrew Reed, remarked:—“I have
already, in a public speech, addressed to a crowded
meeting in the Father Mathew Hall, expressed my full
concurrence in the proposals, the very moderate pro-
posals as I regard them, that are put forward by you
and your Committee. It is unnecessary for me to re-
peat what I then said. Many things that have since
occurred—most notably, the disregard of the healthy
public opinion of the city by the members of the
licensed trade in keeping open their houses of business
on St. Patrick’s Day—have strengthened my conviction
that we have nothing to rely upon but legislation as an
cffective weapon in the struggle against some of the more
serious evils of intemperance in this country.” There
were several very interesting speeches delivered, and
Lord Aberdeen replied sympathetically : —“ As to legis-
lation,”” he said, “he would say'that he was glad to
observe that those who spoke had apparently quite
understood that the present Government were not in a
position, and did not profess to be in a position, during
the present session to bring forward legislation, but it
had been inferred and had been distinctly stated by the
Prime Minister not so long ago, that the Government
might be trusted to be in no wise slack or negligent, if
they were spared long enough, in taking active ;teps in
regard to this question.” The “ Liberal > Government
aro too busy with the English Education Act and other
things to deal with the temperance needs of Ireland.
In the meantime, when may we expect that Tim Har-
rington and Pat O’Brien will be drummed out of the
Trish Party for blocking the St. Patrick’s Day Bung-
closing Bill?  May we ask again—why do not the Party
make the St. Patrick’s Day Closing ‘a Paxty question,
and so snuff out Tim and Pat, the doughty champions
of the spongers on St. Patrick? .

. There is close on a two-column review of Tom 0’ Kelly

\\'()bx-t;:mo fum:‘hns snfd that the objeot of every novel and avery othor
butlern AJ.”:\S well should be to make the world brighter and

‘.Y‘L’Idgcd lzy’ this standard we beliove Mr. D. P. Moran’s novel,

Tom O’Kelly,” ‘would not be found wanting. Wo don’t know
Whether Mr. Moran would bo flattered by being compared to Zoln
in his treatment of his subject, but the impression loft on our mind
by this most interasting book now beforo us, is that the author is
a sort of Irish Zola, a writor with all the virtues and nono of tho
Vices of tho great French novelist. The book is one to hold the
attention of tho rcader, and if the reader is at all impressionable,
to hold it spell-bound from the fivst pago to the last. At the samo
time ﬂmhlwo say this we have no doubt but the reador of tho
\\‘all;mmmn:Tv but quick transit or shallow type will riso up from
 perusal of the book with a feeling of despair for tho future of
Ireland. But Ivishmen or Irishwomen with any good in them will
bo all the better for having their oyes opencd by the reading of
this book; and though the snobs, scamps, and sots of Bally-
fown " almest monopolise the stage and are permitted the fullest
and freest liberty to make asses of themselves, still occasionally we
get a glimpie of the solid, w. rthy and industrious folk who con-
stitute after all the great majority of the people everywhere in this
country, and we are made to feel that though th» veneor of Ballytown
is false, its heart is sound enongh. The reading of “Tom O’Kelly”
by the youth of Ireland is calculated not only to do away with all
un-Irish tendencies, but to dispel the falso notions of patriotism,
which, like a noxious parasite, have settled upon the country and
choked out much of what is good and true in the national life of
Ireland.

Mr. Moran, in the Leader, ha id c0o many downright truthful
things of the Irish Press individually and collectively that few
journalists in this country are broad-minded enough to {reat this
book on its merits. Most of them have studiously ignored it.
Had Mr. Moran been an Englishman and had he written with
equal a vitriolic attack on Ireland and Irishmen, or had
he been = priggish Englishman, who, with the best intentions, but

utire ab-once of good taste, penned an apologia for tho
sh u:tion, ke would in either case be quoted by the yard in
every newspaper in Ireland, and he would be held up by many
as a heaven-inspired genius. Bearing in mind these facts, one is
tempted to say it is a pity tho author did not have the book
published anonymously. As a fatherless child it would make its
way more specdily in the world of literature. Buf, then, it is a
hook which could have been written by only one man, and that
man is the editor of the Leader.

We are, however, very much mistaken if, notwith:tanding this

does not yet find its way into every

cre the people are not entirely illiterate. In his

y of stating home truths, Mr. Moran is not at

all unlike Thackeray. Like him he unceremoniously tears the mask
off humbugs of every class.

Further down in our contemporary’s review we read—

It has been remarked of Dickens, as showing his commanding
genius, that he haa woven round a poor little workliuse foundling,
“OQliver Twist,” a story of surpassing human interest. In “Tom
O’Kelly,” the central figure is a very commonplace every-day
youth, but the book itself is a veritable human document that will
be poruzed by many generations of unborn Irishmen, who, let us
hope, will wonder at the extraordinary mental condition of the
people of the lust years of the nincteenth century.

And again :—

The reader follows the fortunes of the O’Kelly family with rapt
attention, and as (ho characters in fhe book are marhalled on the
stage, one feels that he has known them all his lifo, as, indered, he
has. Who that has ever read the Cork Ezaminer will say he hae'nt
scen the Ballytown Fcho? Who doesn’t know tho Royal Hotel,
with its highly intellectual society?

We take the following from the last paragraph of the
review :—

Space does not permit us to deal more fully with the volume.
We have just given a few extracts to excite the readors’ interest,
for whom Mr. Moran has written a book that is not only entertain-
ing and instructive, but a book that will romain one of the most
prominent landmarks in tho history of the growth of Trish com-
mong¢ense. The gift of ridicule with which the author has heen so
largely endowed has been used unsparingly in the volume before us,

Mz, William Wilkinson, Secretary of the All Treland
Bazaar Committee, writes us with reference to the pro-
ceeds of that enterprise. Ie says:—“ As we have many
enquiries in regard to the Balance Sheot of tho All Ive-
land Temperance Bazaar, will you kindly allow me space
to say that I have this day sebtled the last claim, and
roceived what I expect is the last outstanding amount
to credit. The Books and vouchers are in the hands of

the official auditor, S. Cameron Potter, who hope_s to
have the balance shect, etc., ready in a very short time.
£3,000 has been handed to each of the two Societies on
behalf of the funds of which the Bazaar was organised
—The Irish Temperance League and The Irish Associa-
tion for the Provention of Intemperance. These
Societies have paid off the debts largely contracted by
financing the Irish National Temperance Executive,
which is representative of all classes and creeds in Ire-
land; they have further placed a sum at the d\sppsal
of that Executive for furthering Temperance Catering,
Temperance Clubs, and other counter attractions.
From this sum grants have been made to Committtees
in Counties Clare, Tipperary, Galway, Carlow, VV'ex-
ford, Cork, Waterford, Dublin, Limerick, Leit‘r!m,
Cavan, Sligo, Kilkenny, Westmeath, and Kmg’s
County ; other applications are awaiting the formation
of local representative Committees, when they will be
considered.’’

Our readers were already made aware that a grant
had been made for use down Athy district, where
a “Saved ”’ Professor of Music was employed to lecture
on temperance in that rather “Idolatrous’’ district.
We anxiously await a perusal of the balance sheet.
Has the lucrative post, rendered vacant by the retire-
ment of Mr. Moriarty, been filled yet? and if so, is
Mr. Moriarty’s successor to the Secretaryship of this
“all creeds, all classes’ organization also “Saved?’
Mr. Moriarty is a bankrupt, with liabilities over £2,000,
and assets about £200. If Mr. Moriarty had been an
“TIdolator,”” how the critics of the “ priest-ridden >’ sec-
tion of the people would moralise. There is no dis-
guising the fact that the financial collapse of Mr.
Moriarty is a serious blow to the Irish Association for
the Provention of Intemperance, a hody in which we
never had unlimited confidzace.

This Association wants looking after like every other
body, and we will keep our eye on it. What is the
Association doing with the balance of the £3,000 that
it received from the Bazaar? We notice that £3,000
of the “all creeds, all classes >’ money was given to the
Irish Temperance League of the North, and that League
has an official organ, and that official organ, about a
year ago, contained an article in which there was a most
insulting reference to the ““Idolatrous’’ religion. We
hope that any of our readers who may have any de-
tailed and interesting information concerning the work-
ing of the Association, or the Temperance League, will
write us.

The unfortunate thing in Ireland with reference to
Temperance reform is that the Irish Parliamentary
Party appear to act as if they were in the hollow of M.
Bung’s hand. If that Party threw itself into the cause
of Temperance Reform, a great deal could be done.
But so terrified of Bung are the majority of the mem-
bers of it that we find Pat O’Brien and Tim Harrington
are allowed to block the St. Patrick’s Day Closing Bill,
whilst we know that if the Party made this patriotic
measure a Party question, the opposition of such
Bungites as Pat O’Brien and Tim Harrington would be
at once suppressed. Wo don’t suppose Pat O’Brien
would defy tho Party even to please Mr. Bung.

Wo may as well suggest again—for one has to keep
pegging away ab theso matters—bthat a resolution con-
demning the wasteful and demoralising Drink Bill of
Ireland should be proposed at every National political
convention in Ireland. And might we suggest, also,
that tho Church Street Fathers should prepare a resolu-
tion, or a serics of them, have them passed at some
meeting of the Church Street body, and send them
around to every Branch of the United Irish League,
and every Urban and Rural District Council in Ireland.
Branches of the U.IL.L., and our various local govern-
ment bodies are past masters in the gentle art of passing
resolutions. Well, why should not Church Street give
them a good bracing anti-Bung resolution to pass.
There is really, we submit, great propagandist poten-
tiality in this suggestion; if various Bung-streaked

THE LEADER.

Councils and Bung-dominated branches of the U.I.L.
bolted at the resolutions, {here would be, even still, tem-
perance propaganda in the very bolting of Bung, and in
the records of it in the various provincial papers.

It will be remembered that wo suggested in a recent
issue that Lady Aberdeen should exp%'%ss a wish that at
the next outdoor society function over which she pre-
sided those attending should be clothed in materials of
Irish manufacture. In the following issue we pointed
out that we had learned that our suggestion was not a
new one, that, in fact, twenty years previously Lady
Aberdeen had actually attempted such an entorprise.
We notice that she has thrown out a hint that she would
like those who attend the Coik Show in J: uly, at which
she will be present, to be dressed in suits of Ir’ish manu-
facture. We do not put our trust in princesses, not to
talk of mere countesses, and, we suppose, most people
at this Show who will take heed of Lady Aberdeen’s
advice will be Seomini; but, no doubt, many Irish
woollen mills will benefit somewhat by this attempt to
popularise Irish manufacture amongst the  beastly
jolly, don’t you know,” circles. In some cases, no
doubt, the poor tailors will be a long time, if not for all
time, out of their money; but a movement in favour of
using Irish goods even amongst the Seomini is good and
desirable in itself. After all, the Seoinini are there,
and as unhappily we cannot drown them, in consequence
of moral and physical difficulties, we must make the best
of them. Many of the Seomini are not half bad, and
the poor creatures cannot help having empty heads. If
they take to Irish industry to the tune of “ God Save the
King,” well, better that than that they should be chant-
ing “God Save the King’’ in British tweed. You
must sometimes lift a Seommi tenderly, or he might
break; take him, by easy stages, as far on the Irish
road as you can. If Brother Goulding attends this
function, he will have to buy his cigarettes in some place
other than his railway refreshment room, for nothing so
common as Irish cigarettes are stocked there.

We have before us a copy of the programme, souvenir,
or whatever it may be called, of the Viceregal Garden
Party in May, 1886. The booklet is reprinted from the
Freeman! You see though Irish manufacture was hinted
at twenty years ago in a half-hearted sort of a way, and
though the Freeman wrote up this Viceregal Garden
Party, the Irish manufacture movement was let die out.
The Freeman was only moving in the matter like a gal-
vanised frog, and as soon as the stimulus died its interest
in the matter died also. We wonder how much even of
talk would there be now of supporting Irish industry
if the LeaDER had given up, say, four, or even three,
years ago? Indeed, if it let go to-day the Dark Bro-
thers would be rubbing their eyes in a short time, and
complaining that no one was looking for their caves.
There has been a great stir in Irish manufacturing
circles within the last six years. The movement came
at a time when the country was rapidly going down-hill,
s0 that to arrest industrial decay was formidable enough,
not to talk of developing, to any degree, the industrial
activities in Ireland. We wish every success to any
efforts to make Irish-manufactured goods popular in all
circles, including the most ridiculously “classy” and
the most virulently anti-Irish.

On April 29 the Dripsey Woollen Mills received a note
from the Postmaster, Edgeworthstown, Co._ Longforq,
informing them that “a parcel addressed in Irish is
lying at this office undelivered owing to its being ad-
dressel in Irish. Please send proper address that it may
be forwarded.”” The Dripsey Woollen Mills replied : —
“Yours to hand informing us that a parcel addressed
—— cannot be delivered owing to its being ad-
dressed in Trish. Well, all we can tell you is, that we
have addressed all our letters to our customer as above,
and all have been delivered. ‘Why do you now get your
back up, and cause great, delay and inconvenience? Weo
don’t know any address but the one our customer gives
us, and if you decline to deliver parcel after already

HE very name, “ The Department,’”” has begun to

jar on the ears of this long-suffering and peren-

It is a “ highly respectable '’ no-good; and the sooner

offective shape the better. Sir Horace is clinging to it
like a barnacle.

The proceedings at the recent meeting of the Council
were, in some respects, sensational. Sir Horace’s
speeches made weary reading. He is played out, and
the country is tired of him. It is true that the  Liberal’”

same. The flattery which his servants of the Homestead
sometimes beslaver him with is little short of indecent ;
yet evidently poor Horace swallows it all with a relish.

THE BORDERLAND.
HE Gaelic Leaguer presents many points of re-
semblance to the carly Christian. You ’meeb him
in the most unexpected places, even in Ceesar’s house;
but, on the other hand, his creed is likely to place upon:
him many inconvenient restrictions, and upon occasion
hamper considerably his intercourso with the heathen.
What a world of trouble his unwillingness to
throw a few grains of incense on Cwsar’s altar,
or to put in a formal appearanco at those reli-
gious rites in which no one really believed must have
caused our Christian forefathers. For the imperial
Roman was eminently tolerant, but he never could be
brought to sce what difference a handful of incense could
mako to anyone. So, 0o, the Gaelic Leaguer, and for
tho purposes of this article I will understand by the
term anyone who is unwilling to increase the hold of
Anglicisation on Ireland, not infrequently finds himself
brought up straight against the stone-wall of his prin-
ciples. But I fear that unlike the early Christian, per-
haps becauso we do not look forward to as vivid a reckon-
ing for our Gaclic sins in the next world, we, most of us,
compromise a little. And as the extent to which we can
merit in compensation by speaking Irish is not always
as greab as it might be, perhaps it might be as well to

consider the exact nature of these sins.

The great trouble is with what I may term the border-
land. There are some things which are entirely ana-
thema, and no one is in any doubt about them. The
question arises rather in regard to such minor troubles
as cricket, Rugby and Association football, hockey,
tennis, golf, waltzing, barn-dancing and English music.
From a more political point of view, we have a crescendo
of “ God Save the King,”” the King’s Health, and Loyal
Addresses.  Most people can add a few others to the
liss. Now the most annoying about these worries is that
in regard to many of them, at any rate, no definite deci-
sion has been arrived at. There is a strong expression of
opinion every now and then, but the line is not, in
general, drawn with sufficient clearness, and too much is
left to the individual conscience. It is nowhere laid
down authoritatively that you may not play cricket,
but may play golf, or that you may be present at “ God
Save the King,”” but not at the King’s Health, or may
be present at the King’s Health, but not present at a
Royal Address, or yet that all these things are, in all
cases, wrong. No doubt the Irish Party takes the dis-
tinction that you may drink the King’s health, but may
not, propose a loyal address, though even to this rule it
scarce adheres rigidly. But it is the only case I know
of a line being definitely drawn.

Now let us start with English games. I have very
often heard it urged as a most unreasonable thing on the
part of the Leaper and the Gaelic League, that they
should start an opposition to amusements so healthful
and harmless as cricket and Rugby football. Surely it
was urged there is nothing very wrong in a young fellow
taking a little necessary exercise in this way. He is
none the worse Irishman because he does so. Now if a
man were to spend a small portion of his time, with no
particular influence on his life, playing foreign games, it
would not do him or his country much harm, any more
than if he were to devote a portion of his time to a
foreign language. But that is not how the question
arises, and we know, in fact, that foreign games are
seldom played by an Irishman without their sapping
overy Irish enthusiasm. How many cricketers shud;
Irish? Go down to Pheenix or Pembroke and try. How
many men play both hurley and hockey, or both Irish
and English football?  Anglicisation so far resembles
sin that is seems impossiblo to give way to it in modera-
tion.  Weo might pardon an odd game of cricket ox
bockey, but such a case as a man otherwise Irish indulg-
ing in only nn‘odd game never arises in practice. S

Of course, it might be answered that this is becauso
oven the occasional sinner is forthwith excommunicated.
and beco;nes hardened in crime, but that is not the reai
explanation. It is not mere accident that the pupils of
Clongowes, Castleknock and Blackrock are seb to pla;
cricket rather than hurley. Nor can it be argued, \vitlb;
any honesty, that it is owing to the intrinsic merits of

;

one game over the other. Talk all the humbug you like
a;bouf: the qualities of endurance, science, otc., developed
by cricket ; do you know many Irish past cricketers, by

the way, famous for endurance or scientific
attainments? and when you are dono yon
know in  your . heart of hearts that that

is not the real reason at all. Desiring to speak with all
fairness, it is impossible to bo blind to the fact that it
simply becauso it ¢s an Irish game that hurley is not
pla.y?d, just as it is because it 75 an Irish dance that the
zeel is not danced in Dublin middle-class drawing-rooms.
Make as many pleas and pretences as you like, and you
kuow_ very well that is the true explanation. There is
a prejudice against Irish amusements as there is against
Trish manufactures. They are thought not to be smart,
and not to be respectablo, just because they are Irish.
Yet what a pleasure it is to see, as I often do, the young
boys in the Co. Limerick and tho Co. Cork playing
lurley, and then, turning to a story such as that in this
mounth’s New Ireland Review, sco that the same game
was played by young Cuchulain. What a grand history
there is in this; what a scandal that it be sacrificed for
a passing fad of English respectability. Now I do not
play hurley. I am afraid that, like many an Irish Ire-
lander, I fulfil my duties negatively in this regard.
Compelled, sore against my will, to play cricket and
other inflictions at school, I signalised my advent to
citizenship by the abandonment of all games whatso-
ever. I simply could not muster up enough vital
energy for hurley or Gaelic football at present.  There
are several nice middle-aged English games—golf,
tennis, etc.—for which I could muster up the energy
were they permitted. I have refrained, and I fear I am
developing a self-sacrificing, patriotic adiposity in conse-
quence. Undoubtedly such games have some degree of
Anglicising influence, but there is nothing Irish to take
their place. I think some official line should be taken
in this matter. They should either be permitted, or
some effort made to supply their place.

Another and more difficult matter which occurs in all
these borderland questions is the social element. Man
being a social animal, but not always a Gaelic animal in
an equal degree, it is necessary to live our lives among
people, many of whom have little: sympathy with our
principles. Inmany such cases one is practically obliged
to give way to a certain extent. Would life be worth
living at all for the country bank clerk
who refused to join the tennis club?  Then
again it is an open secret by this period
of history that dancing is not the sole reason which
brings people to dances, and under such pressing circum-
stances can you scrutinise the programme too carefully?
Again, coming to a later period of life, if, considering
the King’s health to be wrong, you decide to avoid
dinners where it is proposed, that, in the present decline

of Mansion House hospitality, would be almost equiva-
lent to a resolution to abandon public dining altogether.
How dire would be the fate of the after-dinner speaker
who developed strong Nationalist principles? How far
l are these rules cast-iron? How far should they be?

How far may expediency enforce a compromise? We

want a law-giver and a moralist. CHANEL.

I
T is often said that the Irish, being Celts, are au 
emotional }Jeople. 
'Tis truer to s,1,y that the Irish 
1verc emotional. 
But the saying having had a good 
start, and the world hating nothing more, than being 
forced to re-examine its now easily-handled ideas, we 
may not expect that phrnse, "the emotional Irish," to 
be knocked on the head for some time. Yet the phrase 
is not altogether. devoid of truth, ~he Irish being, even 
at the p1·esent time, more suscepti-ble of emotio·n than 
the people of England. 
England, however, is cold-
blooded : the frivolity of its amusement, the state of 
its arts, its reJ.igious condition-examining England with 
r~gard to _any of the emotional e~onomies of a people's 
hfo, one is forced· to the conclusion that the blood of 
that body-national is run to water, if. not to w:.orse. 
An excess of the riches of this world has thinned the 
blood of England; and the lack of these self-same riches 
has thinned, and is thinning, tha.t of Ireland-the same, 
r,i:sult, different causes. 
•

Perhaps racially the Celt is more emotional than tho
Ten}ton ; but for all that we believe that there were his-
torical causes behind that richness of emotion, that ful-
ness of heart, that gentlenoss of heart, not only per-
ceived, but dreamt on with love by everyono whom the
Gaelic League has enabled to gaze into the life that
existed in old days in Irish Ireland behind its penal
fastnesses. Yet for the thistorians that have been, not,
thank God, for those that axe, this people, our kin, were
as a people that dwelt in a mist. Only now can we form
somo idea of the big things in their lives: they had a
life apart from that of the atrophied Irish mnation;
worldly riches and kindred thoughts were not for them ;
an “eastern world’’ existed in their poems, and to them
the real worlds beyond sea were not less visionary;
out of their circumscribed life they wove melodies of
‘words and sounds strangely sweet, and what were thus
added to what the ages had given them were valued by
them, for no hatred could circumscribe the ficlds of the
mind, and not even “law’’ itself could rob them of
such harvests; and though, indeed, their dwelling may
be said to have been in a vale of tears, partings were un-
common, and their sorrows were not of that cataclysmic
nature which stifles sorrow. Given such conditions, an
emotional life could not but eventuate. Such secem to
us the reasons of that softness of character remarked in
the Irish peasant of a hundred years back. Reasons
similar, but microcosmic in comparison, it is which give
that touch of femininity, so characteristic, to the faces
of those we call men of genius.

But time sweeping away the old conditions, the pea-
sant fell into the hands of leaders (great their praise)
one after another, who disciplined him until at last he
became a man of war, tenacious of purpose because per-
fectly acquainted with the ends for which he fought.
In the beginning his softness of character, his emotional
nature, was both help and hindrance—help, for it put
passion into the warfare, and made leading easy—hin-
drance, for it made slow the acquiring of that requisite
hardness which, indeed, came to the peasant only in
our own time. But this political agitation, while
toughening the man, would not account for his latter-
day appearance. There came famine and emigration.
Then the blood began to thin. Tears were set flow-
ing; and when eyes run tears day after day there comes
one day surely when the eyes will shed no more what-
ever sorrow befall; the trouble then becomes cataclys-
mic, and a faculty is in process of being lost. Add to
this the fact that it was really the flower of the nation
.emigrated : for it sometimes happened, nay often, if
it were not the rule, that ome of a large family
was chosen, sometimes solf-chosen, to stay at
home; the rest went. He remained to inherit
the property. Now of what character was he? In
a word, the least adventurous! He who it was thought
would be least likely to waste the inheritance, either by
investing or experimenting, was the one chosen to stay
at home. The stickiest! The woodenest! So the
orator says right when ho speaks of the going of the
flower—the luscious, the fruitful. Is he not also right
when he speaks of the blood-drain? Blood really is the
word.

Acting with these causes there was a third—a most
unlovely and unemotional system of education—most
unlovely, as everyone who came in contact with it, as it
existed up till lato years, knows. It appeared dry
enough in all conscience to the English-speaking child,
tbut to the Irish-speaking child coming from a home where
epics may have been recited at hightfall, it must have
stayed the whole growbh of his mind when in spring
“ the yrannous breathing of the North shakes all our
buds from growing.’’

No one can deny the stifling effect of these great
causes on the Irish heart—the political movements in
connection with ‘the land agitation, the emigration
“trouble, the unnatural education; yet it not uncom-
monly happens that the whole blame is put on the

7] HAT ablo and indefatigable Passionist Father—the

Rev. Arthur Dovine—has deservedly achieved a
high reputation among living Catholic writers for the
many valuablo works he has given to the public. ,H‘S

read, not only in English-speaking countries, but '3/150‘

His latest work on tho  Ordinary of the Mass’ is a.
veritable mine of information on the great Christian
sacrifice, which is the centre and essence of religious wor-
ship on earth. It concerns itself entirely with the cere-
monies of the Mass—with the prayers, the rubrics, t.he
vestments of the priest—in a word, with that magnifi-
cent liturgy that has grown around the sacrifice—like
a sacred and worthy setting that enchases and commends
tho ““ pearl of great price’’ that has been bequeathed to
the Catholic Church by her Divine Founder.

The Rev. Author takes us step by step through the
entire sacred rito—we accompany the priest from “ amice
to amice ”’—from the moment he puts on the sacred
vestments until he puts them off. Every prayer and
every act is explained in that peculiarly simple and in-
telligible style that Father Devine has made his own.
He has given us the history of each prayer and of each
ceremony—as far as it is preserved in authentic sources
—together with their liturgic value, and he has taken
care especially to dwell upon their dogmatic and moral
sense, and their various mystic -significations.

From this brief résumé of its contents, one feels jus-
tified in asserting that Father Devine’s book is one of
the most important contributions to Catholic literature
of the present day. It is a volume that ought to be in
the hands of every intelligent Catholic, for it cannot
fail to impress him with a deeper and holier apprecia-
tion and veneration for the highest act of his religion,
and for the eloquent and majestic ceremonial in which
it is enshrined.

I don’t know how far one could go in recommending it ta
Irish Protestants, seeing that they give little countenance

niously rejected as ““idolatrous.”’” The results of latter-
day historical research have now made it palpably evi-
dent that in rejecting the Mass as “idolatrous’’ the
Reformers broke away from the constant practice of the
Christian Church from apostolic days, and blotted out
from their “ New Religion’’ what Christian antiquity
ever regarded as the essential rite and worship of the
Catholic Church. Such is the position of Protestantism
to-day—a position that is brought home to it by the
ablest Protestant scholars of our time—such as Dr. Har-
nack, of Berlin, and Sabatier, the late Dean of Protes-
tant Theology at the Sorbonne. The results of modern,
scientific, historical criticism bring into clearer light the
unity and continuity of the dogmas of the Catholic
Church—and as regards the Mass in particular—that
the great sacrifice which Catholics assist at, and which
their priests offer to-day, is essentially the same as that
which St. Justin described as the practice of the Chris-
tian communities of the second century, and has the
same essential note as that which St. Paul celebrated at
Troas. .

When T referred to the G.A.A. catering for the youth
of Ireland, I was well aware of all recent developments ;
but, as everyone will admit, plenty of cup-ties and
championships for the youth are not sufficient. Clubs
cannot exist without money, and they cannot flourish
except they get substantial monetary support from the
people or from the Association. It would be interest-
ing to know the financial position of the leading clubs in
the city and county of Dublin at present. Most of
them could do with financial help.

My, O’Toole is wrong in thinking the pioneer Soccer
club had either wealth or influence. It had many years
‘of hard struggle both for popularity and financial sup-
port, but by dint of careful and intelligent management
it is now in a very strong position, financially and other-

thousand people at Jones’s Road, but after deducting the
